reasoning: The question resolves YES if there is at least one single day in a 42-day period (May 20 to June 30) where the word "tariff" or "tariffs" is absent from the front pages of BOTH the New York Times and the Wall Street Journal. For this to resolve NO, the word must be present on at least one of the two papers' front pages every consecutive day for 42 days. Newspapers cover a wide array of topics, and even pervasive news themes like trade wars fluctuate with the news cycle, experiencing weekends, holidays (like Memorial Day), or periods dominated by other major events (international crises, domestic politics, natural disasters). Additionally, WSJ does not publish a traditional Sunday print edition, potentially leaving NYT as the sole required page to omit the word on those six days. Relying on an uninterrupted 42-day streak of front-page coverage of a specific policy word is statistically highly improbable. Given these factors, the probability of at least one omission day is overwhelmingly high.
